request.question: What company published both The Scorch Trials and The Death Cure?

title: The Maze Runner (series)
The Death Cure
World Publishing Company
The Scorch Trials
Toby Press
List of role-playing game publishers
Lewis Marnell
Gutsoon! Entertainment
Maze Runner: The Scorch Trials
Maze Runner: The Death Cure
sentences: The Maze Runner is a series of young adult dystopian science fiction novels written by American author James Dashner.

The series consists of "The Maze Runner" (2009), "The Scorch Trials" (2010) and "The Death Cure" (2011), as well as the two prequel novels, "The Kill Order" (2012) and "The Fever Code" (2016), and a companion book titled "The Maze Runner Files" (2013).
The Death Cure is a 2011 young adult dystopian science fiction novel written by American writer James Dashner and the third book, fifth chronologically, in the "Maze Runner" series.
It was published on October 11, 2011 by Delacorte Press and was preceded by "The Maze Runner" and "The Scorch Trials" and followed by the series prequels, "The Kill Order and The Fever Code."
The World Publishing Company was an American publishing company founded by Alfred H. Cahen.
Originally headquartered in Cleveland, the company later added an office in New York City.
The company published genre fiction, trade paperbacks, children's literature, nonfiction books, textbooks, Bibles, and dictionaries, primarily from 1940 to 1980.
Authors published by World Publishing Company include Ruth Nanda Anshen, Michael Crichton, Simone de Beauvoir, Robert Ludlum, Sam Moskowitz, Ayn Rand, Rex Stout, Gay Talese, and Lin Yutang.
The company's Cleveland headquarters were located in the Caxton Building.

The Scorch Trials is a 2010 young adult post-apocalyptic dystopian science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner and the second book, fourth chronologically, in "The Maze Runner" series.
The novel was published on September 18, 2010 by Delacorte Press.
It is preceded by "The Maze Runner", and followed by "The Death Cure".
A was released on September 18, 2015 by 20th Century Fox.
Toby Press was an American comic-book company that published from 1949 to 1955.

Founded by Elliott Caplin, brother of cartoonist Al Capp and himself an established comic strip writer, the company published reprints of Capp's "Li'l Abner" strip; licensed-character comics starring such film and animated cartoon properties as John Wayne and Felix the Cat; and original conceptions, including romance, war, Western, and adventure comics.
Some of its comics were published under the imprint Minoan.
Some covers bore the logo ANC, standing for American News Company, at the time the country's largest newsstand distributor.

This is a list of companies that have produced tabletop role-playing games, listed in order of the year that the company published its first role-playing game-related product (game, supplement, or magazine).
Also listed is the years the company was active, and a list of notable role-playing games the company has produced.
This list makes note of the first edition of each game which a company published, and does not try to list subsequent editions of the same game published by the same company.
Lewis Kristian Marnell (3 December 1982 – 18 January 2013) was a professional skateboarder from Melbourne, Australia who was Slam Magazine's 2008 "Skater of the Year".
Marnell died in January 2013, following complications related to type 1 (juvenile) diabetes, a condition that was diagnosed when he was 10 years old.

Numerous tributes were published following Marnell's death and his longtime skateboard deck sponsor, Almost Skateboards, continues to use the hashtag "#LewisMarnellForever"—on 15 and 29 July 2014, the company published the hashtag with 2006 video footage of Marnell skateboarding in Japan.
Gutsoon!
Entertainment, Inc. is a now defunct publisher of English translated manga.
The company, headquartered in Encino, Los Angeles, served as the US subsidiary of Coamix, which was founded by Nobuhiko Horie and manga artist Tsukasa Hojo.

The company published the English manga anthology "Raijin Comics", a U.S. equivalent to the Japanese "Weekly Comic Bunch" and published many of the same titles ("Fist of the Blue Sky" and "City Hunter"), as well titles from other magazines such as the Japanese "Weekly Shōnen Jump".
"Raijin Comics" began as a weekly magazine with its first issue (dated December 18, 2002), but switched to being a monthly publication beginning with its 36th issue (dated September 2003).
Gutsoon!

also published a companion magazine to "Raijin Comics", titled "Raijin Game & Anime" (originally called "Fujin Magazine"), which lasted only 20 issues before being merged into the pages of "Raijin Comics".
The collected volumes of "Raijin Comics" titles were published under the "Raijin Graphic Novels" imprint.
Raijin Comics also has a mascot called "Raijin Maru".
Before the company's fall they were also planning to make a shōjo manga magazine called "Smile Magazine".

Maze Runner: The Scorch Trials (stylized onscreen simply as The Scorch Trials) is a 2015 American dystopian science fiction action thriller film based on James Dashner's novel "The Scorch Trials", the second novel in "The Maze Runner" book series.
The film is the sequel to the 2014 film "The Maze Runner" and the second installment in "The Maze Runner" film series.
It was directed by Wes Ball, with a screenplay by T.S. Nowlin.

Adding to the original film's cast of Dylan O'Brien, Kaya Scodelario, Thomas Brodie-Sangster, Dexter Darden, Alexander Flores, Ki Hong Lee, and Patricia Clarkson, the new supporting cast includes Nathalie Emmanuel, Giancarlo Esposito, Aidan Gillen, Jacob Lofland, Barry Pepper, Rosa Salazar, Lili Taylor, and Alan Tudyk.

Maze Runner: The Death Cure (also known simply as The Death Cure) is an upcoming American dystopian science-fiction action thriller film directed by Wes Ball, based on "The Death Cure", the final book in "The Maze Runner" trilogy, written by James Dashner, with a screenplay by T.S. Nowlin.
It is the sequel to the 2015 film "" and the third and final installment in the "Maze Runner" film series.

candidate.summarize1.predict: Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: What company published both The Scorch Trials and The Death Cure?
hop1_docs: The Scorch Trials | The Scorch Trials is a 2010 young adult post-apocalyptic dystopian science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner and the second book, fourth chronologically, in "The Maze Runner" series.  The novel was published on September 18, 2010 by Delacorte Press.  It is preceded by "The Maze Runner", and followed by "The Death Cure".  A was released on September 18, 2015 by 20th Century Fox.

The Death Cure | The Death Cure is a 2011 young adult dystopian science fiction novel written by American writer James Dashner and the third book, fifth chronologically, in the "Maze Runner" series.  It was published on October 11, 2011 by Delacorte Press and was preceded by "The Maze Runner" and "The Scorch Trials" and followed by the series prequels, "The Kill Order and The Fever Code."

The Maze Runner (series) | The Maze Runner is a series of young adult dystopian science fiction novels written by American author James Dashner.  The series consists of "The Maze Runner" (2009), "The Scorch Trials" (2010) and "The Death Cure" (2011), as well as the two prequel novels, "The Kill Order" (2012) and "The Fever Code" (2016), and a companion book titled "The Maze Runner Files" (2013).

Maze Runner: The Scorch Trials | Maze Runner: The Scorch Trials (stylized onscreen simply as The Scorch Trials) is a 2015 American dystopian science fiction action thriller film based on James Dashner's novel "The Scorch Trials", the second novel in "The Maze Runner" book series.  The film is the sequel to the 2014 film "The Maze Runner" and the second installment in "The Maze Runner" film series.  It was directed by Wes Ball, with a screenplay by T.S. Nowlin.  Adding to the original film's cast of Dylan O'Brien, Kaya Scodelario, Thomas Brodie-Sangster, Dexter Darden, Alexander Flores, Ki Hong Lee, and Patricia Clarkson, the new supporting cast includes Nathalie Emmanuel, Giancarlo Esposito, Aidan Gillen, Jacob Lofland, Barry Pepper, Rosa Salazar, Lili Taylor, and Alan Tudyk.

Brabender plastograph | The "plastograph", or Brabender plastograph, is a device for the continuous observation of torque in the shearing of a polymer with a range of temperatures and shear rates.  The generic device records lubricity, plasticity, scorch, cure, shear and heat stability.
Scorch (comics) | Scorch is a character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.

Anglo-Saxon Amulets and Curing Stones | Anglo-Saxon Amulets and Curing Stones is an archaeological study of amulets, talismans and curing stones in the burial record of Anglo-Saxon England.  Written by the Australian archaeologist Audrey Meaney, it was published by the company British Archaeological Reports as the 96th monograph in their BAR British Series.  Prior to writing the work, Meaney had published several books dealing with Anglo-Saxon burials.
response.hop2_query: Did Delacorte Press publish both "The Scorch Trials" and "The Death Cure" by James Dashner? source: publisher information (Delacorte Press)
hop2_docs: The Death Cure | The Death Cure is a 2011 young adult dystopian science fiction novel written by American writer James Dashner and the third book, fifth chronologically, in the "Maze Runner" series.  It was published on October 11, 2011 by Delacorte Press and was preceded by "The Maze Runner" and "The Scorch Trials" and followed by the series prequels, "The Kill Order and The Fever Code."

The Scorch Trials | The Scorch Trials is a 2010 young adult post-apocalyptic dystopian science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner and the second book, fourth chronologically, in "The Maze Runner" series.  The novel was published on September 18, 2010 by Delacorte Press.  It is preceded by "The Maze Runner", and followed by "The Death Cure".  A was released on September 18, 2015 by 20th Century Fox.

The Eye of Minds | The Eye of Minds is a 2013 teenage science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner, and the first book in "The Mortality Doctrine" series.  The book was first published on October 8, 2013 by Delacorte Press and is set in a futuristic world where a young gamer must help stop a rogue hacker intent on causing mass destruction.

The Kill Order | The Kill Order is a 2012 young adult dystopian science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner and published on August 14, 2012 by Delacorte Press.  It is the first prequel book in "The Maze Runner" series and the fourth installment overall.  The book is set prior to the events of "The Fever Code" and 13 years before "The Maze Runner" book.

The Fever Code | The Fever Code is a 2016 young adult dystopian science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner and published on September 27, 2016 by Delacorte Press.  It is the second prequel book in "The Maze Runner" series and the fifth installment overall.  The book is chronologically set in between the events of "The Kill Order" and immediately before "The Maze Runner" book.

The Maze Runner | The Maze Runner is a 2009 young adult post-apocalyptic dystopian science fiction novel written by American author James Dashner and the first book released in "The Maze Runner" series, although it is the third chronologically.  The novel was published on October 7, 2009 by Delacorte Press, an imprint of Random House, and was made into a 2014 major motion picture by 20th Century Fox.

Boys Against Girls | Boys Against Girls is the third book in a series of children's books by Phyllis Reynolds Naylor which began with "The Boys Start the War".  It was published by Dell or Yearling Books and originally by Delacorte Press in 1994.  Both hard cover and paperbacks were published, and it is now available as an ebook.
response.summary_2: Delacorte Press (an imprint of Random House)
